content.text:
```json
{
  "signature": "Fugue 20 in A minor is defined by its restless chromatic descent: a subject that winds downward by half-steps from the very first bar, entering successively in soprano (bar 1), alto (bar 3), tenor (bar 5), and bass (bar 8) before embarking on a wide harmonic journey through E minor (bar 13), C major (bar 21), F-sharp minor (bar 33), and D minor (bar 41) — all reached through smooth diatonic pivots that make each new key feel inevitable rather than surprising. The fugue's architecture tightens dramatically from bar 57 onward, where stretto entries pile the subject on top of itself in close imitation across five voices, building tension through repeated half-cadences at bars 69 and 79 before the five-voice coda (bars 80–87) delivers its single, definitive perfect authentic cadence on beat 3 of bar 87. That final cadence — marked as the only PAC to close the whole piece in A minor — lands with the weight of everything that has been withheld.",
  "significance": "The chromatic descent in the subject gives this fugue an ache that never fully resolves until the very last bar, as though each phrase is always leaning forward, always one step away from home — which is exactly why the ending, when it finally arrives, feels both inevitable and earned. The long delay of full harmonic closure, held back by half-cadences deep into the final pages, means every listener senses the tension even without knowing a single musical term.",
  "connection": "The immediately adjacent Fugue 19 in A major (BWV 864b) shares not only the pitch centre A but Bach's characteristic habit of exploring the full circle of closely related keys before returning home — the same instinct that drives Fugue 20's arc through E minor, C major, F-sharp minor, and D minor. Where Fugue 20 leans on chromaticism and shadow, Fugue 19 tends toward the brighter, more diatonic character that A major naturally invites, yet both fugues appear to reflect Bach's practice of pairing adjacent pieces in the WTC as complementary mirrors: one major, one minor, one open, one searching. Playing the two back to back reveals how the same contrapuntal architecture — sequential episodes, key-region excursions, stretto intensification — can carry entirely opposite emotional atmospheres depending on whether it is anchored in a major or a minor key.",
  "connection": "The immediately adjacent Fugue 19 in A major (BWV 864b) shares not only the pitch centre A but Bach's characteristic habit in the WTC of moving through a constellation of related keys before returning home — the same instinct that drives Fugue 20's arc through E minor, C major, F-sharp minor, and D minor. Where Fugue 20 leans on chromaticism and shadow, Fugue 19 inhabits the brighter, more diatonic world that A major invites, yet both fugues appear to reflect Bach's deliberate pairing practice: each a complement to the other in mode and mood. Playing them side by side reveals how the same contrapuntal architecture — sequential episodes, stretto intensification, a delayed final cadence — can carry entirely opposite emotional atmospheres simply by shifting from minor to major."
}
```

I notice I accidentally duplicated the "connection" key. Here is the corrected, valid JSON:

```json
{
  "signature": "Fugue 20 in A minor is defined by its restless chromatic descent: a subject that winds downward by half-steps from the very first bar, entering successively in soprano (bar 1), alto (bar 3), tenor (bar 5), and bass (bar 8) before embarking on a wide harmonic journey through E minor (bar 13), C major (bar 21), F-sharp minor (bar 33), and D minor (bar 41), all reached through smooth diatonic pivots that make each new key feel inevitable rather than surprising. The fugue's architecture tightens dramatically from bar 57 onward, where stretto entries pile the subject on top of itself in close imitation across five voices, building tension through repeated half-cadences at bars 69 and 79 before the five-voice coda (bars 80–87) delivers its single definitive perfect authentic cadence on beat 3 of bar 87. That final cadence — the only PAC to close the piece in A minor — lands with the accumulated weight of everything that has been withheld.",
  "significance": "The chromatic descent gives this fugue a persistent ache that never fully resolves until the very last bar, as though each phrase is always leaning forward, always one step away from home — which is exactly why the ending, when it finally arrives, feels both inevitable and earned. The long delay of full harmonic closure, held back by half-cadences deep into the final pages, means every listener senses the tension even without knowing a single musical term.",
  "connection": "The immediately adjacent Fugue 19 in A major (BWV 864b) shares not only the pitch centre A but Bach's characteristic WTC habit of moving through a constellation of related keys before returning home — the same instinct that drives Fugue 20's arc through E minor, C major, F-sharp minor, and D minor. Where Fugue 20 leans on chromaticism and shadow, Fugue 19 inhabits the brighter, more diatonic world that A major naturally invites, and the two appear to reflect Bach's deliberate pairing practice: each a complement to the other in mode and mood. Playing them side by side reveals how the same contrapuntal architecture — sequential episodes, stretto intensification, a withheld final cadence — can carry entirely opposite emotional atmospheres simply by shifting from minor to major."
}
```
